title: Planning and Presenting Your Autobiography
explanation_chunks: Imagine you're writing a special story about your own life, called an autobiography. This page helps you plan a new chapter, perhaps about an exciting adventure or an unexpected emergency you've experienced. Think about what happened and how you felt, almost like drawing a comic strip to map it out.

When you write, try to use strong, 'precise' verbs that show exactly what happened, like 'sprinted' instead of 'ran'. Also, add 'adverbial phrases' to describe *where* and *when* things happened, making your story super clear. For example, instead of 'I ran fast', try 'I sprinted *through the tall grass* *at dawn*'.

Don't forget to include sounds, like 'CRASH!' or 'whisper', to make your story come alive. And for the most thrilling parts, use short, punchy sentences. These really grab your reader's attention and make the moment feel more dramatic, like 'Quickly I stopped!'

After you've written your chapter, you'll practice reading it aloud in a group. One friend might listen for where and when things happen, another for those impactful short sentences, and someone else for the most exciting parts. This helps everyone improve their writing and speaking skills.
examples: Instead of 'She walked to the shop', use 'She *strolled* to the shop *down the bustling street* *this morning*' to be more precise.
For impact, say 'The monster roared!' instead of 'The monster made a loud noise' followed by a long description.
key_points: Plan autobiography chapters with a storyboard.
Use precise verbs, adverbial phrases of time and place, and sound effects.
Employ short, simple sentences for dramatic impact.
Practice reading aloud and identifying literary devices in group work.

vocabulary.definition: a story someone writes about their own life.
strong verbs that show exactly what happened, like 'sprint' instead of 'run'.
groups of words that tell you more about when, where, or how something happened (e.g., 'in the garden', 'after lunch').
making a strong effect on someone.

explain_again: This page is like a guide to writing your own exciting life story! You learn how to make your story super clear and exciting by choosing strong words, telling where and when things happen, and using short sentences for big moments. Then, you read it aloud to your friends, and they help you see what worked well and what you can do even better!
fun_analogy: Writing an autobiography is like building with LEGOs. You gather all the best blocks (your memories), choose special, colourful ones for details (precise verbs and adverbial phrases), and add a big, exciting explosion block (a short, impactful sentence) for the most thrilling parts. Then you share your amazing creation!
